fields.title: Campus Skill Swap Project
fields.summary: A Django web platform that connects students to swap skills peer-to-peer and register for community-led classes, turning idle expertise into a campus-wide learning marketplace.
fields.business_problem: Most college campuses have an enormous amount of untapped peer expertise. Students who could teach Excel, guitar, calculus, or design, and others who'd happily learn, but no structured way to connect them. Existing tools like Discord servers and group chats are noisy, not searchable, and don't track who's offering what. Campus SkillSwap solves this by giving students a single platform to list skills they can teach, browse what their peers are offering, and register for classes in just a few clicks.
fields.tools_used: Python, Django, SQLite, HTML, CSS, Bootstrap, JavaScript, Django Authentication, Git, GitHub
fields.key_features:
User registration and authentication with secure password handling
Profile pages where users can list the skills they can teach and want to learn
Searchable directory of skills offered across the campus
Class registration system that prevents double-booking and overcapacity
Many-to-many relationships between users, skills, and classes
Responsive interface that works on phones and laptops
Admin panel for moderating users and listings
fields.role_contribution: Solo project from concept to deployment. I designed the database schema, built every Django view and template, set up authentication, wrote the registration logic that handles class capacity and conflicts, and styled the entire UI with Bootstrap. I also handled version control with Git and managed the project as a real product.
fields.biggest_challenge:
Modeling the relationships between users, skills, and classes. A user can both teach and learn multiple skills, and a class has one teacher but many students, so I needed several many-to-many relationships in Django without creating ambiguous queries. My first attempt used a single "Skill" model linked directly to users, but it broke down when I tried to query "all classes I'm registered for as a student" vs. "all classes I'm teaching." 

I refactored to use explicit through-models for the registrations, which made the queries clean and let me add fields like registration_date and status later. That experience taught me to think hard about the data model before writing views.
fields.lessons_learned:
Schema design pays compounding interest. The hour I spent re-modeling the user-skill-class relationships saved me days of patching queries and templates downstream.

Django's built-in features cover more than I expected. Building for someone other than yourself changes the design. Once I started imagining a non-technical student using the site, I cut three "developer-friendly" features that were actually just clutter.

Version control is for solo projects too. Even working alone, branching saved me when I broke the registration flow trying to add a feature so I just rolled back.

fields.title: Syllabus Bot
fields.summary: A no-code AI chatbot trained on a course syllabus that answers student questions instantly — built to explore how non-developers can deploy domain-specific AI assistants.
fields.business_problem: Every semester, professors and TAs answer the same syllabus questions hundreds of times: "When's the final?" "What's the late policy?" "How many absences am I allowed?" Students often hesitate to email for "small" questions, which means they either guess or miss critical policy details. I wanted to test whether a focused AI chatbot, trained on a single document, could handle these repetitive queries instantly, freeing instructors for higher-value conversations and giving students a 24/7 reference they'd actually use.
fields.tools_used: Chatbase, OpenAI GPT, Prompt Engineering, RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation), PDF Document Processing
fields.key_features:
Trained on the full syllabus for MIS 4V98. Chatbase ingests the PDF and chunks it for retrieval
Custom system prompt that constrains responses to syllabus content (reduces hallucination)
Natural-language Q&A interface, students ask in plain English, not keyword search
Suggested questions on launch ("What is the AI policy?", "How many days can I miss?") to lower friction
Mobile-friendly chat UI deployed via Chatbase's hosted widget
Conversation reset button so students can start fresh queries
Powered by GPT under the hood; same conversational quality as ChatGPT, scoped to one document
fields.role_contribution: Solo project. I selected Chatbase as the platform after evaluating a few no-code AI tools, uploaded and prepared the syllabus document, wrote the system prompt that defines how the bot should behave (always cite the syllabus, refuse off-topic questions, recommend emailing the professor for edge cases), and tested the deployment with realistic student questions. I treated it like a small product launch rather than a tutorial: defining who the user was, what success looked like, and where the bot would fail.
fields.biggest_challenge: Constraining the bot's behavior. Out of the box, Chatbase + GPT will happily answer ANY question, even ones unrelated to the syllabus. So a student asking "What's the capital of France?" would get a confident answer that had nothing to do with the class, which is the opposite of what an instructor would want. I solved this by writing a tighter system prompt: explicitly telling the bot to refuse off-topic questions, always reference the syllabus, and direct students to email the professor for anything ambiguous. I also discovered that prompt phrasing matters more than I expected; small wording changes ("only answer using the syllabus" vs. "primarily answer using the syllabus") had big effects on whether the bot stayed on-task.
fields.lessons_learned:
No-code AI tools are real products, not toys. Chatbase did 90% of what a custom RAG pipeline would do, in 1% of the time. Knowing when to use these tools vs. building from scratch is itself a skill.
Prompt engineering is the actual product. The model and platform are commodities; the differentiator is the system prompt that defines behavior, tone, and guardrails.
Scope is a feature, not a limitation. A bot that handles one syllabus well is more useful than a bot that tries to handle everything poorly. This applies to almost every AI product.
The biggest gap in my version is upload permissions. Right now, only I can upload syllabi, which means it doesn't scale.

fields.title: Handyman Service Automation Agent
fields.summary: An end-to-end automation that turns a handyman customer request into a priced quote and scheduled appointment using a multi-agent AI workflow — replacing 30 minutes of manual phone tag with a 2-minute
fields.business_problem: Small service businesses like handymen lose hours every day on the intake side of their work — fielding calls, asking the same questions, ballparking quotes, checking their calendar, and following up by text or email. Every minute spent on intake is a minute not spent fixing something. I built this workflow to automate the entire lifecycle from "customer submits a request" to "customer gets a quote and proposed appointment time," so the handyman can focus on actual work instead of admin. The whole thing runs without the business owner touching their inbox.
fields.tools_used: n8n, Google Forms, Google Sheets, Gmail API, OpenAI GPT, Webhooks, REST APIs, Multi-Agent Orchestration
fields.key_features:
Google Form intake — customers submit job details (type of work, location, urgency) through a public-facing form
Auto-trigger on row addition — a new Google Sheet row instantly kicks off the entire workflow
Data normalization step — cleans and structures raw form input before passing it to downstream agents
Intake Agent — uses AI to extract and classify the job (e.g., "leaky faucet" → plumbing, low-complexity)
Pricing Agent — calculates a quote based on job type, scope, and pre-defined rate logic
Scheduling Agent — suggests an available appointment window
Comms Agent — drafts a personalized customer email with the quote and proposed time
Automatic Gmail dispatch — final email sent to the customer with no human in the loop
End-to-end response time under 2 minutes from form submission
fields.role_contribution: Solo project from concept to deployed workflow. I designed the entire architecture — deciding which steps needed to be AI-powered (judgment calls like pricing and communications) versus deterministic (data normalization, sheet writes). I built and connected every node in n8n, configured the Google Sheets trigger and Gmail integration, set up the four AI agents with their own scoped prompts, and tested the workflow end-to-end with realistic handyman job submissions.
fields.biggest_challenge: Designing the separation of concerns between agents. My first version used a single AI call to handle intake, pricing, scheduling, and email drafting all at once — which sounded efficient but produced inconsistent results. The pricing logic would leak into the email tone, the scheduling reasoning would drift, and debugging was nearly impossible because everything happened in one black-box prompt. I refactored into four discrete agents, each with its own narrow prompt and a single responsibility. That meant more nodes and slightly more complexity in the workflow graph, but the outputs got dramatically more reliable, and I could now tweak one stage (like pricing logic) without breaking the others. It taught me that in AI workflows, modularity beats cleverness almost every time.
fields.lessons_learned:
Multi-agent design is a real engineering tradeoff, not a buzzword. Splitting work into specialized agents gave me reliability, but cost me latency and complexity. Worth it for this use case; wouldn't be for simpler ones.
Deterministic steps belong outside the AI. Things like "write to a spreadsheet" or "format a phone number" should never go through an LLM — they're brittle and waste tokens. The AI should only handle the parts that require judgment.
The hardest part of automation isn't the automation. It's modeling the messy real-world process well enough that automation makes sense in the first place. I had to think through what a handyman actually does manually before I could decide what to automate.
This pattern generalizes. The same structure (Form → Intake Agent → Domain Agents → Comms Agent → Email) could power lawn care, tutoring, dog walking, freelance design — any service business with a repetitive intake-to-quote process.

fields.title: ACE Suit MK1 — AI-Generated Defense Tech Marketing Campaign
fields.summary: A full B2B marketing campaign for a fictional defense-tech product, built end-to-end with structured AI prompts driving 3D product renders, magazine covers, brand assets, and marketing copy — a study
fields.business_problem: Marketing teams now have access to generative AI tools that can produce renders, photography, copy, and brand assets in minutes — but most teams still produce generic, low-quality output because they don't know how to direct the models. The gap between a vague prompt and a usable commercial asset is enormous, and most users fail at the prompt-engineering layer rather than the tool layer. I built this project as a structured pitch deck for a fictional defense-tech product (the ACE Suit MK1) to test whether disciplined, structured prompts could produce visually coherent, on-brand marketing assets across multiple modalities — 3D renders, hero photography, magazine covers, marketing copy, and logo design — for a single product.
fields.tools_used: Google AI Studio, Generative AI Image Models, Prompt Engineering, Structured Prompt Frameworks (Context / Objective / Style / Audience / Response), Brand Strategy, Marketing Copywriting, PowerPoint
fields.key_features:
Full B2B marketing pitch deck for a fictional defense-tech product (ACE Suit MK1) with target audience identification (U.S. Armed Forces, tactical units, military medical teams)
Detailed product specification covering ballistic protection, load distribution, power management, environmental seals, haptic feedback, and fail-safe mechanisms
3D cross-section product render — split-image realism on the left half, engineering wireframe on the right, with strict single-object rules and lighting controls
Editorial magazine cover concept — "The World's Metal Man" with photorealistic styling, dynamic posing, and physical magazine staging
Hero shot of a soldier in the ACE Suit — full lighting direction (golden rim light, 35mm composition, 8K detail) and environmental context (snowy halfpipe base)
Group photograph composition with multi-subject staging and luxury rooftop environment
Logo design for the parent company "Shock & Awe Inc." with thematic guidance (lightning, slanted typography) and integrated tagline
Structured marketing copy generation using a four-part prompt framework (Context / Objective / Style & Tone / Audience / Response)
fields.role_contribution: Solo project from concept to final deck. I created the fictional product (ACE Suit MK1), defined its target market and feature set, designed the visual brand direction, and wrote every AI prompt across 3D renders, photography, magazine covers, logos, and marketing copy. I treated each prompt as a brief — establishing context, objective, style, audience, and response format — rather than a one-shot text request. The final deliverable is a coherent multi-modal marketing campaign generated entirely from structured prompts.
fields.biggest_challenge: Maintaining visual and tonal consistency across multiple prompts and image types. Each AI generation is statelessly independent — a render, a magazine cover, and a hero shot are separate prompts that don't know about each other, so without disciplined direction, they look like three different products from three different brands. My first attempts produced disjointed outputs: the magazine cover felt like a movie poster, the hero shot felt like a stock photo, and the renders felt like CAD models. I solved this by writing a shared visual system across all prompts — consistent lighting language ("strong golden rim light," "soft natural fill"), consistent composition rules ("35mm wide-angle, center-weighted"), and consistent material direction ("crisp fabric detail with individual threads visible"). The lesson was that brand consistency in AI-generated content is a prompting discipline, not a tool feature — the model won't enforce it for you.
fields.lessons_learned:
Prompt structure beats prompt length. A four-section prompt (Context / Objective / Style / Audience / Response) consistently outperformed long unstructured paragraphs because it forced me to think about what I actually wanted before I asked for it.
AI marketing assets need a brand bible, just like human-produced ones. Without a shared visual system across prompts, output collapses into stylistic inconsistency. The system has to live in the prompt itself.
Constraints generate creativity. Rules like "the dividing line between realism and wireframe must be perfectly vertical" or "render only one object" produced sharper, more controllable images than open-ended prompts ever did.
Generative AI is closer to creative direction than text generation. Writing these prompts felt much more like briefing an in-house design team than writing a search query. Understanding shot types, materials, lighting, and brand positioning mattered more than knowing the model's parameters.
This pattern generalizes to almost any commercial AI work. The same prompt structure that produced this campaign could direct AI for product launches, real estate listings, e-commerce hero images, or political campaigns — anywhere a brand needs consistent multi-modal output at speed.

fields.title: Gleam Mobile Detailing — AI Booking Agent
fields.summary: A LangChain agent powered by Gemini 2.5 Flash that handles the full customer booking lifecycle for a mobile detailing business — quoting, scheduling, and reserving Google Calendar slots through natura
fields.business_problem: Small service businesses lose leads when they can't respond fast enough — a mobile detailer can't pick up the phone while detailing a car. Existing booking widgets are rigid forms, and chatbots are conversational but can't actually take action. I built a LangChain agent that combines the conversational fluency of an LLM with real tool-calling capability, so a customer can quote, schedule, and book an appointment in a single chat thread without the business owner ever touching the conversation.
fields.tools_used: Python, LangChain, Google Gemini 2.5 Flash, Google Sheets API, Google Calendar API, OAuth 2.0, Service Account Authentication, python-dotenv, REST APIs
fields.key_features:
Autonomous tool selection — the agent decides which of three tools to call based on what the customer is asking, with no hardcoded routing
Live pricing tool that reads a Google Sheet of services and multipliers, then uses Gemini to interpret customer requests into itemized quotes
Scheduling tool that connects to Google Calendar via OAuth, reads busy/free time, and returns numbered available appointment slots
Booking tool that creates real Google Calendar events with customer name, service type, location, and notes
Multi-turn conversation memory — customers can quote, check availability, and confirm in one continuous thread without re-explaining context
Confirmation gating — the agent never books an appointment until the customer explicitly confirms, preventing accidental commitments
Two distinct auth flows handled correctly — service account for read-only Sheets access, OAuth desktop flow for write access to Google Calendar
Environment-based secret management with python-dotenv and a defensive .gitignore so credentials never enter source control
fields.role_contribution: Solo project from architecture to working agent. I designed the three-tool structure, wrote each tool as a LangChain-compatible function with proper input schemas, configured both authentication flows (service account for Sheets pricing data, OAuth desktop flow for Calendar write access), wrote the system prompt that defines the agent's behavior and constraints, and tested the full flow end-to-end with realistic customer conversations. I also handled production concerns — confirmation gating, conversation memory, and secret management.
fields.biggest_challenge: Designing the agent's decision-making boundary. Out of the box, a LangChain agent will happily call any tool the user implies — including booking an appointment the customer never confirmed. My first version had this exact bug: a customer would say "I think 2pm works" and the agent would book it, treating "I think" as confirmation. That's catastrophic in a real business — accidentally double-booked customers, calendar collisions, refund disputes. I rewrote the system prompt with explicit confirmation rules and added defensive logic in the tool itself to require a confirmation flag. The bigger lesson was that agents do exactly what you let them do — guardrails aren't optional, they're the product. The code that prevents bad behavior is just as important as the code that enables good behavior.
fields.lessons_learned:
Agent design is mostly about constraints, not capabilities. Adding tools is easy. Stopping the agent from misusing tools is the hard part.
Authentication architecture matters more than I expected. Service account vs OAuth aren't interchangeable — picking the right one for each use case was a real engineering decision.
Tool design is API design. Every tool I wrote is essentially a function the LLM has to read, understand, and call correctly. Clear names, tight input schemas, and good error messages mattered as much as the underlying logic.
LangChain's abstractions are leaky in useful ways. The framework gives you 80% of an agent for free, but the last 20% — confirmation gating, error recovery, edge cases — is bespoke work. Knowing when to extend the framework vs go around it is the actual skill.
This pattern generalizes. The same tool-calling architecture could power tutoring, lawn care, dog walking, freelance design — any service business with a quote-and-book workflow.

fields.title: ML Foundations: Regression and Classification with scikit-learn
fields.summary: A four-part hands-on study of core machine learning workflows — regression, classification, and dirty-data preprocessing — built from the ground up with scikit-learn across four real-world datasets.
fields.business_problem: Most "intro to ML" tutorials stop at one dataset and one model — which is exactly the wrong way to learn how machine learning actually works in practice. Real ML work isn't about training one perfect model; it's about navigating the full workflow on different problem types and adapting when the data isn't cooperating. I built this project as a structured progression through four datasets — each one introducing a new ML challenge — so I could practice the core skills (loading, exploring, training, evaluating, visualizing, cleaning) the way they actually show up in industry.
fields.tools_used: Python, scikit-learn, pandas, NumPy, Matplotlib, Hugging Face Datasets, Jupyter / VS Code
fields.key_features:
Simple Linear Regression on the diabetes dataset — predicting disease progression from BMI with a single-feature line of best fit
Multi-class Classification on the Iris dataset — predicting flower species across three categories with 97% accuracy and a confusion matrix breaking down model mistakes
Multiple Linear Regression on the California Housing dataset — using all 8 features (income, house age, rooms, location, etc.) to predict house prices, with coefficient analysis showing median income as the strongest positive driver
Real-world data cleaning on the Auto MPG dataset — handling missing values stored as "?" strings, converting numeric columns from strings to integers, and dropping non-numeric features that linear regression can't process
Train/test split methodology applied across all classification and multi-feature regression tasks
Visual diagnostics — scatter plots with regression lines, confusion matrices, and residual analysis
fields.role_contribution: Solo project. I built each script from scratch — selecting the dataset, defining the prediction target, choosing features, splitting train/test data, training the appropriate scikit-learn model, evaluating performance with the right metrics for the task type (R² for regression, accuracy and confusion matrix for classification), and visualizing the results. The MPG project required additional ETL work — cleaning string-encoded missing values, converting types, and pruning categorical columns the model couldn't handle.
fields.biggest_challenge: The MPG dataset surprised me — it's the only one of the four with genuinely dirty data, and it broke my code immediately. Some horsepower values were stored as the literal string "?" instead of numbers, and the entire numeric column had been loaded as strings. My first training run threw a cryptic NumPy error that didn't tell me anything about why. I had to learn to actually inspect the data before training — running `.dtypes`, `.isnull().sum()`, and looking at sample rows before piping anything into a model. Once I built that habit, the fix was straightforward (replace "?" with NaN, convert to numeric, drop or impute missing rows, remove non-numeric features), but the bigger lesson was that the *modeling* part of ML is the easy part.
fields.lessons_learned:
The full ML workflow is way more than just .fit() and .predict(). Loading, exploring, cleaning, splitting, training, evaluating, and visualizing are seven distinct steps, and skipping any of them produces unreliable results.
Inspect data before training. .dtypes, .isnull(), and .head() should always come first. Most "ML errors" are actually data errors in disguise.
Match your metric to your task. Regression problems need R² and residuals; classification problems need accuracy and confusion matrices. Using the wrong one tells you nothing.
Coefficient signs are interpretable. In the California housing model, seeing that median income had the largest positive coefficient was a sanity check — it matched the real world. Models that don't pass that gut check are usually broken or biased.
Real-world data is messy by default. Clean tutorial datasets are training wheels. The MPG project taught me more than the other three combined because it forced me to handle the ugly parts.
